title: A Practical Guide to Building Simple LLM Workflows(For When Agents Are Overkill)
author: Jyotsna Choudhary
text:
A Practical Guide to Building Simple LLM Workflows(For When Agents Are Overkill)
Generative AI has brought a paradigm shift across nearly every industry by redefining the way software works, interacts, and enhances business productivity. The general capabilities of LLM, when augmented with enterprise data itself, have been so impressive in solving so many different problems like automating workflow, democratizing knowledge, content creation, etc.
And the next big wave the industry is witnessing, though still in early phase, is the Agentic AI, which essentially means- AI systems autonomously making decisions to complete task(s) by interacting with their environment. While its capabilities are immense and it presents a vast amount of opportunity for another paradigm shift in futuristic AI, it’s equally important to know when not to use it, else it can bring unnecessary complexity, unpredictability, and even chaos into business workflows. This becomes especially evident in multi-agent setups, where multiple AI agents collaborate or compete to achieve a goal, which can often lead to complex behavior, harder debugging, and increased coordination overhead.
Challenges with Multi-Agent Systems
While multi-agent systems (MAS) offer flexibility, their intrinsic complexity can be overkill, especially for simple tasks. One of the papers I found on this[1] presents a comprehensive empirical study about this issue and says that MAS often performs marginally better or worse than single-agent or simple best-of-N methods. They evaluate the failures of even state-of-the-art MAS like ChatDev with merely 33.33 % correctness.
They have identified 14 failure points categorized into three broader failure modes: (i) specification issues, (ii) inter-agent misalignment, and (iii) task verification. Below is the figure presenting the failure distribution among three different categories. It’s also emphasized in the paper that the failures shouldn’t be attributed to mere LLM limitations like hallucination or superficial prompt engineering failures, but rather could be due to flaws in the design and agent coordination.
It’s also adviced in the Anthropic’s blog -Building effective agents that an LLM solution should always start with the simplest solution first, and more oftent than not, agent is not the simplest solution!
This brings me to the point that most of the business problems nowadays can be solved using LLM workflows. In simple words, an LLM workflow is a well-defined, guided flow of steps where a single LLM or a group of LLM can be employed to complete a task through the deterministic path, executed with thoughtful actions by making use of tools LLM has at its exposure and through structured output and conditional checks.
It’s presented in Anthropic’s blog I mentioned above, as well as in the LangGraph Documentation, with practical examples for different types of workflows. I’d strongly recommend going through it — https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/tutorials/workflows/
Here, I am going to show a quick guide using a small use case- where I will build a workflow for a small case study in order to understand how efficient a simple workflow can be.
The case study is — Build a solution that can generate a few startup ideas related to a specific domain, and then validate each idea through market research to make an informed decision about whether to pursue it.
Here, I will be using LangGraph to create a workflow, but there are various other frameworks, packages available in the market that could be explored as well. So, the prerequisite for understanding the following section would be general familiarity with LLM application-related concepts and LangChain.
So, first thing is we would need to design a workflow for the problem statement we presented above, which could look something like this:
Now, let’s try to implement this workflow using LangGraph. The first step is defining a state of the graph, which is nothing but the schema of the graph. It has pre-built reducer functions, which handles update to the states.
class State(TypedDict):
user_input: str
messages: Annotated[list, add_messages]
domain: str
startup_ideas_list: List[str]
Next, we define three different nodes for the three main components of our workflow — idea generator, idea validator and final decison maker.
i) startup_ideas -> This will be the first chain in our workflow, which simply invokes an LLM to generate three startup ideas related to the input domain. Here, we have formatted the LLM response by binding it with a structured response output model so that our response contains parsable output and is free of unnecessary fluff.
class StartupIdeas(BaseModel):`
startup_ideas: List[str] = Field(
description="List of Generated startup ideas")
def startup_ideas(state: State):
print("---human input----")
domain_input = state["user_input"]
structured_llm = llm.with_structured_output(StartupIdeas)
ai_msg = structured_llm.invoke(f"Generate 3 startup ideas related to domain: {domain_input}. Just List the ideas. Don't provide a description or additional information on ideas. Just the ideas which are self-descriptive.")
return {"domain": domain_input, "startup_ideas_list": ai_msg.startup_ideas, "messages": ai_msg.startup_ideas}
ii) validate_ideas — This chain is responsible for deciding and selecting tools that our workflow should execute to gather all the research work on the ideas generated in the first step.
def validate_ideas(state: State):
domain = state["domain"]
startup_ideas = state["startup_ideas_list"]
llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools(tools)
validator_prompt = validator_prompt_template.invoke({"idea": f"{startup_ideas}", "domain": f"{domain}"})
ai_msg = llm_with_tools.invoke(validator_prompt)
print(f"decided tools to be used are: ", ai_msg.tool_calls)
return {"messages": ai_msg}
Here, the LLM is bound with certain market research tools, and each tool can help with a specific type of research for the ideas and related domain. So, what the LLM outputs here is not the tool’s results, but rather which tools to call based on what it sees in the tool’s description. Hence, it’s crucial to define the tool’s description while defining it.
Here are the tools I have created(You can always add more tools to the list):
- Competitor Finder: Find competitors for a domain.
- Legal Risk Checker: Check legal risks for a domain.
- Market Size Estimator: Estimates market size and CAGR for a domain.
- Risk Score Calculator: Calculate the total risk score for an idea.
- Trend Finder: Find trends for a domain.
Here’s the implementation of one sample tool:
Note that here I have simply created a mock implementation of the actual research. To do the actual research on it, you can add a relevant API in the else condition (For example, Tavily search API, or anything else), but the mock implementation works for us for now, since the focus is on realizing a workflow.
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from typing import List, Tuple
@tool(response_format="content_and_artifact")
def competitor_finder(domain: str, mock: bool = True) -> Tuple[str, List[str]]:
"""
Find competitors for a domain.
If mock=True, returns fake competitors.
"""
if mock:
competitors = {
"healthcare": ["MediTech", "HealthFirst", "CareWell"],
"fitness": ["FitTrack", "GymSmart", "MoveWell"],
"education": ["EduBright", "Learnify", "SkillUp"]
}
response = competitors.get(domain.lower(), ["GenericCompetitor1", "GenericCompetitor2"])
content = f"Competitors for domain {domain} are: {response}"
return content, response
else:
# Future: Call real competitor lookup API
raise NotImplementedError("Real API integration not implemented yet.")
Check out the rest of the tools in my GitHub code here.
Once, this node is executed, the output looks something like this:
decided tools to be used are: [{'name': 'market_size_estimator', 'args': {'domain': 'fitness', 'mock': True}, 'id': 'call_J0gL2Wzs5eG6vTwdMiPaMWUN', 'type': 'tool_call'}, {'name': 'competitor_finder', 'args': {'domain': 'fitness', 'mock': True}, 'id': 'call_MfilZyIHoYYuJXkCsyRTtTbt', 'type': 'tool_call'}, {'name': 'trend_finder', 'args': {'domain': 'fitness', 'mock': True}, 'id': 'call_eACd80YDS2k1xQIs2ZRCTBgV', 'type': 'tool_call'}, {'name': 'legal_risk_checker', 'args': {'domain': 'fitness', 'mock': True}, 'id': 'call_zvZSxjuqENQ2wMvggLfxmxj0', 'type': 'tool_call'}, {'name': 'risk_score_calculator', 'args': {'idea_details': 'Virtual Reality Fitness Classes App', 'mock': True}, 'id': 'call_KscYAJvr3FXJdIC1PTeQZl3D', 'type': 'tool_call'}, {'name': 'risk_score_calculator', 'args': {'idea_details': 'AI-Powered Personal Trainer Wearable', 'mock': True}, 'id': 'call_MmZnCFziCivWWVLa5j8jpPxI', 'type': 'tool_call'}, {'name': 'risk_score_calculator', 'args': {'idea_details': 'Fitness Challenges & Rewards Platform', 'mock': True}, 'id': 'call_DQNIxQwJs0pMNoKNzEJu5HOw', 'type': 'tool_call'}]
Note, that it correctly identifies all the relevant tools that should be invoked to get a complett view of market data in order to evaluate the ideas related to input domain.
Now, assuming that our workflow calls all these tools, the next step would be to evaluate the feasibility of each of these ideas and present the user with a first line of opinion based on the market research data.
so, let’s create the final node, which invokes LLM to make decisions about each of the ideas.
def decision_maker(state: State):
market_research = state["messages"][-1].content
domain = state["domain"]
startup_ideas = state["startup_ideas_list"]
decision_maker_prompt = decision_prompt_template.invoke({"idea": f"{startup_ideas}", "domain": f"{domain}", "market_research": f"{market_research}"})
ai_msg = llm.invoke(decision_maker_prompt)
print("Decision maker response:: ", end="\n")
print(ai_msg.content)
return {"messages": ai_msg}
So, great, there we have all the nodes defined, now all we need to do is create a graph with all these nodes and define the edges between them.
builder = StateGraph(State)
builder.add_node("startup_ideas", startup_ideas)
tools = [competitor_finder, legal_risk_checker, market_size_estimator, risk_score_calculator, trend_finder]
tool_node = ToolNode(tools=tools)
builder.add_node("tools", tool_node)
builder.add_node("validate_ideas", validate_ideas)
builder.add_node("decision_maker", decision_maker)
builder.add_edge(START, "startup_ideas")
builder.add_edge("startup_ideas", "validate_ideas")
builder.add_conditional_edges(
"validate_ideas",
tools_condition,
["tools", END]
)
builder.add_edge("tools", "decision_maker")
builder.add_edge("decision_maker", END)
graph = builder.compile()
Note that, tool is itself a node in the graph which would be invoked according to the conditional edge, based on “tool_condition”, which is a pre-defined condition that checks if the source node wants to call tools. If the condition satisfies, then it invokes the tool node or the workflow ends.
The rest of the edges are defined according to the workflow diagram we saw previously. Now, to visualize this graph, we can run the snippet below.
from PIL import Image as PILImage
from io import BytesIO
img_bytes = graph.get_graph().draw_mermaid_png(max_retries=5, retry_delay=2.0)
img = PILImage.open(BytesIO(img_bytes))
img.show()
This shows a graph like below:
Voila! Our graph is compiled and ready to serve user requests. We can either do it directly by looping through the events in graph.stream(), or we can create a simple UI out of it using Streamlit like this:
import streamlit as st
from orchestrator.graph import graph
from typing import List
st.set_page_config(page_title="Startup Idea Validator", layout="centered")
st.title("🚀 Startup Idea Validator")
st.markdown("Generate and validate startup ideas using agentic workflows powered by LangGraph.")
with st.form(key="idea_form"):
domain = st.text_input("Example domains: education, healthcare, fitness, etc.", placeholder="Type a domain here...")
submit = st.form_submit_button("💡 Generate & Validate Ideas")
if submit:
if not domain.strip():
st.warning("Please enter a domain to proceed.")
else:
with st.spinner("🔄 Running LLM validation process..."):
st.markdown("---")
st.markdown(f"### ✅ Startup Ideas for domain: `{domain}`")
for event in graph.stream({"user_input": domain}):
for value in event.values():
msg = value["messages"]
if hasattr(msg, "content"):
st.markdown("**🛠 Tool Output**")
st.write(msg.content)
else:
st.write(msg)
st.success("✅ Idea generation and validation complete.")
and then when you run this file called app.py with the command streamlit run app.py
You see a UI like below:
Here’s my GitHub code for you to run this yourself, and feel free to enhance this further. I would love to hear how you expand it by adding more cool functionalities.
Also, here’s a video below if you’d like to see a quick demo of the application before running it yourself.
So, this was a wrap. This was just an example to show that you can build many such workflows without having to build an agentic system for your use cases. The pre-defined workflows offer consistency, reusability, simplified debugging, performance tracking and feasible optimizaton and hence easier to prodcutionize.
In the race to build intelligent multi-agent LLM systems, it’s easy to get caught up in complexity. But as recent research shows, many of these systems fail not because the LLMs are weak , but because the workflows are poorly designed. Before reaching for autonomous agents, consider whether your use case truly requires them. In many cases, a structured workflow with clear task boundaries and simple tool orchestration can be far more robust, interpretable, and production-ready.
Nevertheless, we can’t overlook the advantages that agentic systems bring. In use cases where workflows are highly dynamic and require automated decision-making at multiple steps or branches, agents are the way to go. I’ll try to follow up with another blog soon that dives deeper into agents and their applications.
If you found this blog helpful, feel free to like, share, and subscribe for more content like this. I’d also love to hear your feedback and thoughts on agents and workflows. Thank you! ✨😊✌️
